transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Joe, you couldn't be reading our mind more.

I saw it after we got in here.
Either that or back of a part of a data book or something.
I hate like the devil to tear one of those other fenders off.

And the reason tape won't stick is that everything's got a fine coating of dust, and the only way I could finally get it to stick was to put tape on it (and then) rip the tape off - or take the tape off - which took some of the dust off and then (another piece of) tape would tend to hold it.
But it just won't hack it up here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger, Gene.
That's exactly what we're thinking (see below).
And what we're going to do is run through the fix in a pressure suit a few hours from now.
And if it looks like we can do it, and it won't cost you many more than say 10 minutes, we're going to have you go through with it.
If it takes longer than that, we're going to go back to the drawing board and see what else we can do here.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Well, you know John (Young) and Charlie (Duke) can tell you just how bad it is.

But, man, just that short trip back from where we lost it, and we were just covered.
The whole...I couldn't even read parts of the panel on the Rover, plus (there was a lot of dust on) all the battery covers and everything.

tokens: Okay, guys, you want me to address first those to Jack, and then address them to Gene later on, or you guys both want to answer them at the same time?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Well, we're both listening.
We can answer them.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, the way you asked that, I wasn't sure.
(Pause) Okay.
Question number 1 concerns the Rover mobility rates.
The Rover mobility rates over the short span you drove, which is hardly representative, are slower than people had anticipated.
Do you think this is due to visibility, terrain, or what?
Do you think you can still hack a 7.3 or 8-kilometer (per hour) average to Station 2 tomorrow?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: The answer to that is "yes", Bob.
I think it's partly fam(iliarization), but it's also the fact that we did a lot of circling.
We didn't drive in many straight lines.
Trying to find, for the most part, our bearings, and tried to pick some high spots so we could look around.
So I think straight-line navigation out in the area we're going (tomorrow) is going to be easily 8 kilometers (per hour).
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, great.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Okay.
Another question here, Gene, that you should be able to answer with a simple yes or no.
Was there any spillage of the material in the drill core while you were breaking it down?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Simple "no".
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay.
And...
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Spillage out of it?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Yeah, you know, when you broke the sections, did you lose much material out of it?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: No, sir; I didn't lose any.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, next simple question.
When you were drilling the deep core where the neutron probe was, could you see the RTG over the rock?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Yes.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay.
You have any feel for how high the rock is or how low...(correcting himself) how deep the thing (the top of the neutron probe) was with respect to the RTG?
Were you down in a level that was below (the RTG), even without the rocks being there?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Yeah, I think I...Yeah, I was in a slump.
There was a ridge between us and the RTG, and I had the rock in a line-of-sight between it and where I put that core (hole).
And I'd say the rock was certainly near the ridge and it was - what, Jack?
- I don't know, was it a meter to...(It was a) meter high for the most part.
And it sloped off, and I'd say at least a half a meter high in the line-of-sight from where the neutron probe is to the RTG.
Plus, there's a lot of undulations...I think it'll be below the line-of-sight, anyway.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay.
And a somewhat more general question, here.
It says, and I'll read it, "We're still puzzled as to whether there is a dark mantle.

Could you say something more about the dark regolith surface?" There's a lot of discussion, today, about whether or not it could have been a regolith derived from the intermediate gabbro which you were sampling as boulders.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Bob, I think I don't have too much to add to what I said near the end of the EVA.
I do not have an intuitive feeling that the regolith has been derived from most of the boulders that we're seeing.
Because those boulders are fairly light-colored, they look like they're probably 50 percent plagioclase.
It could be that the regolith is derived from some other material that has blanketed the area.
I don't think we have that answer, yet.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay.
I copy that.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Bob, the boulders we were sampling...

think Jack and I both feel that it's probably...We feel we sampled the subfloor because we saw, in the sides of the craters, where some of these boulders were exposed almost as if they were bedrock down there.
In driving back from what we called Station 1, we could definitely see the light mantle out in the area where the potentials of a slide are.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, very good.

Yeah, I think that the...At least, it's a going bet around here that we're sampling the subfloor when we're sampling - at least the top of the subfloor - when we're sampling the intermediate gabbro there.
The rocks and the boulders.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Okay.
We also...
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Yeah, the...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Bob.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Go.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: It is sort of strange that we don't see a good population of finer-grained rocks.

These rocks look very much like igneous rocks, but they're considerably coarser than comparable...Well, they're about the grain size of some of the coarse-grain Mare basalts that tend to differentiate the crystallobalite and tridymite; but we didn't see any of the finer-grain versions.
If it's an intermediate crystalline rock, we have not seen any fine-grain equivalents yet.
At least not in abundance.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, I copy that.
(Pause) We gather that there's no color change in the dark-mantle material at depth.
In other words, the footprints, wheel tracks, and the rake sample, et cetera, were sort of uniform in color.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: No, there's no major change, but looking out the window - and I think I commented on it - the disturbed regolith (near the LM) is darker.
Oh, I don't know, maybe by 10 percent albedo, something like that, than the undisturbed surface.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, I remember your commenting that when you were walking to the ALSEP, I think, Jack, in fact.
(Pause) Okay, during drilling of the heat flow holes, Gene...
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: (Responding to the first half of Bob's comment) That's right.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: ...was there change in color of the cuttings as they piled up...as you went down in depth?
Do you remember any of that?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Yeah, Bob, both in the core and the heat flow holes, it really didn't seem to pile it up like you're accustomed to at the Cape, and I guess maybe that's because I was kicking so much dust around there.
But I looked specifically when I cleared flutes and what have you, and I didn't see any difference in terms of color, texture, or anything else coming up.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, copy that.
(Pause) On the outcrops you think you see in the North and South Massifs, do they appear to be linear, horizontal or subhorizontal?
Can you see layers, and do you have any feel for the thickness or the attitude or the continuity of them?
Can you discuss these outcrops?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Bob, going over (the site) yesterday (during a pre-landing orbit), I thought I could see a structure dipping off to the southeast, apparent dip anyway, on the eastern side of the South Massif.
Or northeastern side.
We haven't examined them in detail because we were in a rush to get out.
We'll put the binoculars (means "monocular") on them and try to examine that question.

There's nothing very obvious, any more than you can see on the (overhead) photos, that the ledges were concentrated in the upper portion - (coughs) excuse me - in the upper portion of the Massif's units.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay.
Copy that.
Here's a short one that I'll ask Jack since he did it.
Again I guess we'll have to prove this.
(With regard to) the shade portion of the cosmic ray experiment: the question is, and I repeat - I quote - "Are you sure that the detectors, not the decals, were facing out?"
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: I am, Bob; because I said I was sure, and I called you on it.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Roger.
I was sure, too, but I had to ask the question.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: I...I under...I know.
I understand why it was asked, because I did it wrong at the Cape.
But that's why I mentioned it when I deployed it.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Roger.
(Pause) Okay, and we can go and recharge the other PLSS whenever you're ready there, guys.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Okay.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, the next question - which calls for a little bit of discussion - is: The layers of lineaments that you remarked on in the Sculptured Hills, can you say anything about them?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Yeah, Bob, I did.
I think I said...and I commented (that) I'm not sure whether it was the Sun angle or not...But see, I was not looking at the Sculptured Hills; I was looking back at Bear Mountain, I believe.
And, to me it looked like there was some organization that was dipping back to the east, somewhere between, oh, 20 and 25 degrees maybe.
And it was very obvious to me.
But I'm a little hesitant (to say it isn't a lighting effect) because of some of this Sun-angle stuff.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, I copy that.
I gather we didn't get any 500-millimeters of these lineations, today, right?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: No, but I think we will.
They were on the western side of Bear Mountain back there, and I think I commented that I thought that Bear Mountain is probably what the Sculptured Hills look like.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, I copy that.
Is there a scar above the light mantle material?
In other words, the slide.
Is there a scar above that on the South Massif?
Can you see anything up there to indicate that it might have come off of there?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Nothing obvious yet, Bob.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, copy that.
On the way back to Station 1, you described a small crater with light material on the bottom.
Can you say anything more about that crater?
(Long Pause)
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Bob, I don't remember saying that, or...Gene doesn't either.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay.
You talked about something that was light.
I don't remember; I thought it was a boulder, but the question's about a crater.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: You're right.
You're right, there was a large zap pit in a boulder that was very white.
It must have been...The crater for the zap must have been 2 centimeters diameter anyway.
And it had about that, oh, maybe 3-centimeters worth of crushed minerals around it, that gave it a white - very bright white - appearance.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay.
Well that was indeed a small crater, so I guess the question was right.
(Pause) Let me change the mode here and ask you three or four simple ALSEP questions again, to verify for various people exactly what happened.
Just to make sure that they're clear on it.
Jack, when you were laying out the geophone leads, you mentioned and asked me if it was all right if the geophone leads crossed one another, if there was EMI (electromagnetic interference) problems.
And so that made people wonder whether or not it was possible the geophone positions were reversed; i.e., geophone 1 was laid out in geophone 2s' direction, et cetera.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: No, that was just a Geophone 4 problem.
The geophones are in the right directions.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Geophone 4 fell out of the module and rolled under one of the other lines, or vice versa, I don't know which, and it's crossing one of the other lines.
Geophone 1, I think.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, no problem.
When you went to put the LSPE antenna in the heat flow socket, you weren't able to do it at first; was it because of there was a lot of dust in there?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: No, I think it was the same old problem of that piece of aluminum foil or whatever it is going down in the socket and jamming briefly.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, I copy that.
Did you clear out that foil when you did it, or did you just push it on through?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: I forced it.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay.
When you taped the SEP solar cells down, how much of them did you cover with tape?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: We taped the back.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Ah, very good thinking.
And, Geno, a question for you on the Rover when you parked it.
Do you have any feeling for the roll angle it was parked at the LM?
The roll angle.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Yeh, let me look.
(Pause) Bob, it's pretty flat.
If I had to guess, I'd say zero.
And you can bias that by a degree or so, but basically zero.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, is the pitch scale still on it, or did it fall off yet?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: No.
I was going to comment on that.
It's still there.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, very good.
Okay, when you went to Station 1A - we're calling the new Station 1 "Station 1A" - were the blocks there as well-filleted as those near the LM and the ALSEP?
Do they all look the same?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Bob, all the boulders had filleting to a slight degree, but not an extreme amount.
I think it no more than what is being caused by the redistribution of the darker, fine-grained regolith.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, I copy that.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Bob, if I had to answer...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: ...if I had to answer that question, I'd say yes.
Yes, that the boulders are filleted over there about like they are over here.
That would be my impression.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: All right.
Is there any indication that the fillets are directional, in other words, that the fillets are heavier on one side than the other?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Bob, haven't noticed that.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, I copy that.
Do you have the feeling that some boulders are more rounded...
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Well that's a good ...(Hearing Bob) That's a good...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Roger.
I agree with you.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: That's a good re...(Hearing Bob) That's a good reminder, Bob.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, do have any feeling that some boulders are more rounded than others?
Apparently this looked this way in some of the TV pictures.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Some of the big ones that are just barely exposed above the regolith looked quite well-rounded.
Most of those around the craters are subangular.

I got the impression that it's just purely a function of how long the same material's been exposed; but some of the big boulders like the one out near the geophones is quite angular in part and quite rounded on other parts.
It's quite variable.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, do you want to say any more about that boulder (Geophone Rock)?
Did it seem to have more or less the same lithology, in addition to the variation in vesicle size that the other rocks in the vicinity of the ALSEP, and the other rocks out at Station 1 had?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: No, it's very comparable to the ones that we saw at Station 1, as a matter of fact.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, I copy that.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Both types of rocks were there, both variations.
(Pause)
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Do you have a feeling for where the big blocks in the LM/ALSEP area came from?
Do you think they were from Camelot, like (garbled) been saying?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Don't have an idea yet; I'm really not sure.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, and as you drove along on the traverse from the SEP to Station 1, did the size of the small craters with blocky rims vary?
In other words, what we are looking for here is the variation in the thickness of the dark mantle?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: I can't answer that one yet, Bob.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay.

Let me sum up by saying that - I guess as I indicated before - our best guess is that the vesicular crystalline rock - probably gabbro, or I think you've been calling it intermediate basalt or gabbro - forms at least the upper part of the subfloor.

I don't think we've been close enough to a large crater rim to say what the deep sections of the subfloor form; but we think that this intermediate gabbro, vesicular rock - at least medium-grained, perhaps coarse-grained rock - forms at least the upper layer of the subfloor.
Over.
(Pause)
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Yeah, Bob, I think that's pretty safe, right now.
Once again, I'm surprised that it's as coarse as it is, that being the upper portion of a plains unit.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Say, Bob, driving back from Station 1, where we did some of our circling and what have you, we didn't have time to get off, but we did see down in - I don't remember whether it was in the slopes of some craters, or down on the slope itself - but I'd say several meters down below the mantle where there was what we almost agreed to might be bedrock (on) at least a deeper portion of the subfloor.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, well, I think we'll get to it tomorrow.
I think I might just give you a clue to our thinking for tomorrow.
But, I don't think we've seen, or done, anything today that is going to make us change very much from the nominal station...
(correcting himself) EVA-2 plan.
(The next sentence is edited for clarity.) The fact we didn't get to the large boulders at Emory is probably going to mean that Station 5 might be shifted a little bit to the boulders on Camelot.
But certainly Station 5 on the subfloor and also Station 10 have assumed a higher priority than they originally had.
Other than that I, don't think we'll see an awful lot of changes to EVA-2.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Okay, Bob.
I think that's safe.
I suppose somebody's thinking about the possibility of going down to Emory.
Maybe you just said that.
Going down to Emory late in EVA-3.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I think at the moment they're thinking primarily about going to Station 10, and not going to Station 1.
(Pause)
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Okay, Jack, I've wrested control...
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Some of your experts might...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Some of your experts might think about what they might expect to happen to.
What the regolith on a fine-grain pyroclastic would look like.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: We'll tell them.
I'll see you tomorrow, guys.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Sleep well, Bob.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, I've just got one question, Bob, before you run off.
Did the
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: As far as I could tell, Geno, it did.
As a matter of fact, I didn't see the gravimeter people afterwards to talk to them.
But as far as I could tell, it did.
We had one funny reading back at the LM very early when it was on the ground, which I'm at a loss to understand right off.
But other than that everything seems to have gone very well.
The readings were quite uniform in fact, which is what makes me think they went well.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Okay, well, I'd like to leave it...You know it's a little change in my thinking (but) I'd like to leave it on the Rover if we can, although it's a piece of cake to take off.
It's very difficult to lean over that far without losing your balance and take your readings and what have you.
So if we can leave it on (the Rover), it would be far better.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
I was noticing that.
And I also noticed the only three-ball (three-zero) reading we got was when it was on the ground.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Yeah.
(Pause)
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Gene and Jack, if you'll get Lunar Surface Checklist to (page)
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay.
After the line: "Empty ETB As Follows," change the first line which reads, "B&W Mag Golf In Forward RHSSC" (right-hand side storage compartment) to read, "B&W Mag Hotel In LCG Compartment".
And then go into the next column, which begins, "Stow In ETB." Change the second line, which reads: "LMP's Camera With B&W Mag Hotel" to "LMP's Camera With B&W Mag Golf".
That's Mag G, ETB.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Got you.
Hotel, stow it; and go out with Golf.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: That's got it.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: And I've got a LEVA-cleaning procedure which maybe you could pencil in there.
It's an easy three-step procedure.
And I'll go ahead and read it step-by-step here.
Step number 1 is: "Tap LEVA base to remove loose dust".

Step number 2 reads: "If excess dust still remains, use a towel from the LM tissue dispenser, which has been wetted with water, and gently wipe the visor from the top to the bottom; that is, in one direction.
And fold this towel after each wipe to keep the contact surface clean".
There's a note: "Take care not to wet the inside - that is, the concave surface - of the gold visor." And the last step is: "Allow it to air-dry".
And that's it on the LEVA cleaning.
(Pause)
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Okay, Joe, we got that.
The Commander's PLSS has had its final charge, and we're in the process of working on the LMP's PLSS now.
I guess there's no way to verify how much water you've got in there except to go through the procedure.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: That's right, Geno.
And we think you fellows have earned a good meal now, and maybe you can take the rest of the day off.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Okay, Joe.
Thank you.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, Joe.
Just to bring you up to date on (film) magazines.
Mag Bravo has seven-seven frames (exposed).
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay.
(Pause)
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Mag Hotel has eight-three frames.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Jack, on your mag Hotel, we'd showed you all the way up to a hundred and eighty three at one time on that.
Did you miss the "one", this time?
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: I may have clipped it out, Joe.
One-eight-three, yes.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, yeah, you did clip it out, clipped it out cleanly.
So thanks for verifying that.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: (Sounding very congested) Joe, mag Romeo has two-one frames.
And I took a few, random, and probably not very good 500-millimeter (pictures) of the North and South Massifs.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, Jack.
Thank you.
(Long Pause)
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: And, Joe, verify that you want mag Charlie substituted for Mag Bravo on the CDR's camera.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Stand by.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Don't get me wrong.
I think it's a good idea, Joe.
Don't let everybody work all night on that one.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Jack, I think the answer to that is "yes".
Per the checklist, by the way.
That's the way we show it in our checklist here.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Roger.
We probably have about 100 frames left on Bravo, so we'll just keep track of that.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Jack, it'll go out later on, Bravo will.
Apparently, it's kind of your backup magazine there.
(Pause)
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Okay.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: The reason being, we want to start that EVA-2 with a fresh mag.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Hey, Joe.
Bob told us earlier (that) the sounder looked like it was working.
(Long Pause)
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Gene and Jack, just a general comment on that.
SIM bay's cooking along beautifully.
We are getting lunar sounder data.
It looks quite interesting.
We've only got one or two annoying problems with it, but nothing major...That is, with the SIM bay, not with the sounder.
One of them being that we have our usual mapping camera extend problem.
And we've just decided to leave it extended and it will serve it right if it gets a little contaminated with an occasional (water/urine) dump.
And I guess there's a minor problem with one of the big antennas.
It didn't pass its retract check properly, so I guess it may have to be jettisoned when we do a plane change.
Otherwise, things are working beautifully.
Over.
(Pause)
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: That sounds great; I'm glad to hear that.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Houston, Challenger.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: We're sort of around (1)27:30 in the checklist, more or less, and you want the Power Amp and TM (Telemetry) to High?
(Pause)
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Ready when you are.
(Long Pause) And, troops, are you raiding the pantry up there yet?
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Yes, sir.
We've been hitting it as hard as we can.
(Pause) Okay, Power Amp is going to Primary and PCM (Pulse Code Modulator) to High.
(Pause) And, while we're waiting for Gene to look at his computer, shall I do the battery management?
(Pause)
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Jack, stand by - until we get the high bit rate - on that battery management.
And, a reminder, are you still recharging that PLSS number 2 there, or have you taken that off the line?
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: No, we're through with that.
We caught it with 10 minutes.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay.
We've got high bit rate now.
Go ahead with battery management.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Okay, we'll play it...Gene'll work the computer, and I'll work the batteries.
And the ED volts are 37.2, both batteries.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Thank you.
(Long Pause)
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Okay, you got P00 and Data, Joe.
(Pause)
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Thank you.
(Long Pause)
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Okay, Joe, the battery management's complete.
How does the rest of the spacecraft look, what you can see of it (through telemetry checks)?
(Pause)
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, Jackie.
Copy the battery management complete, and the Challenger's looking beautiful from down here.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: I guess you don't have telemetry on dust yet, huh?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Negative on the dust.
And the computer's yours.
Sounds like you've got hay fever sensors, as far as that dust goes.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: It's come on pretty fast just since I came back.
I think as soon as the cabin filters most of this out that is in the air, I'll be all right.
But I didn't know I had lunar dust hay fever.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: It's funny they don't check for that.
Maybe that's the trouble with the cheap noses, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Could be.
I don't know why we couldn't have gone and smelled some dust in the LRL (Lunar Receiving Lab) just to find out.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Goodness knows we've tried.
(Pause)
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Okay, I'll wait for your cue on the rest of it.
(Pause)
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, Jack.
Telemetry PCM Low, and your Power Amp Off, please.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Challenger, this is Houston requesting Down-voice Backup, and then configure your
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Okay, we're working in that direction.
Down-voice Backup, now.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, Jack and Gene.
And, unless you've got questions, or we can help you out in some way, we'll say good night to you.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: (Faint) (Or) good morning.
(Long Pause) The reason I say that, Joe, it's going to be another 30 minutes or so anyway - probably more like an hour - before we actually close our eyes.
(Pause)
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger, Gene.
You think you'll be able to use about 30 more minutes of sleep tomorrow morning?
What's your wish on that?
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Yeah, I'd like to try to get the full amount (of sleep).
As I recall, tomorrow's a little bit flexible.
If we get out 30 minutes late, it doesn't really hurt us.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Sounds like a good way to proceed.
We'll give you the full 8 hours tonight, Geno.
And you do have a time pad in there, so it shouldn't hurt a thing.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Yeah, the big object tomorrow is to get out, and get back in, and the same thing with the next day.
I don't think we're really that time critical either day that we can't go an hour either way.
And I think we'd prefer to have the full 8 (hours of rest) tonight.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
We couldn't agree with you more.
And if there's any way we can be helping you now, just speak up.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: No, you've been doing fine.
We just got a little housecleaning we got to do that's going to take us...I expect we'll be an hour late, Joe.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Hey, Joe.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: (There was) some ambiguity in your statement.
You want us to use a tissue or a towel on that visor cleaning?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Jack, they call it...They call it a "towel", but it comes from the LM tissue dispenser, so I would interpret that to mean "tissue".
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Well, you and I are thinking alike.
But could you ask back there and find out?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Asking right now.
(Long Pause)
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Jack, our guess was right on the cleaning of the visors there.
We're to use a tissue from the LM tissue dispenser.
And I've got an unrelated question for you.
We're chasing (the cause of some apparent) water usage down here.
Could you tell us, please, if you refilled the
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: That's affirm.
We have.
(Long Pause)
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay.
Thank you.
(Pause)
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: We have been drinking quite a bit of water, Joe.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay.
Thank you.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Houston; Challenger.
How do you read?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: You're loud and clear.
Go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Joe, I just took a quick look with the hand lens at that large rock I brought in, and I don't think there's much more than 30 percent plagioclase.
I'll go back to it being more of a standard basalt or gabbro.
It has a fair proportion of ilmenite in it, I believe.
There's some bright platelets - in the vugs or vesicles - of ilmenite.
Now it could be that, if the soil is very glassy, that it's developed the darker color from the contribution of the
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Roger, Jack.
Copy that.
Sounds interesting.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: All it means is that we don't yet know the origin of the dark mantle.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Roger.
(Pause)
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: That rock looks like I may have, by accident, sampled one side of one of the parting planes that I mentioned.
(The rock is) very, very sharply bounded on one side by a planar surface.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Roger, Jack.
Say again.
You may have sampled by accident the side of what?
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: No, I mentioned when I sampled it.
It had one very planar surface, and looking at it more closely, it looks like one of those parting planes that I talked about even earlier in the EVA.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Ah, Rog.
Copy.
"Parting planes", thank you.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: That's like a parting shot (for the night).
(Pause)
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Of which you've been known to have an overabundance, by the way.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Oh, I didn't know that.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: All us fast finishers do.
(Pause)
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: That's right.
You got to figure out what race you're in though, first, Joe.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I'm sure that Sherlock Holmes would have a suitable quotation to answer that, Jack.
I just can't come up with it right now.
(Pause)
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Something like "therein, Watson, lies the problem"...
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: That, in itself, is a singular event.
(Long Pause)
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: But the dog did nothing in the nighttime, Joe.
(Long Pause)
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And when you've examined all possibilities and eliminated all but the very improbable ones, then the improbable one must mean the truth.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: I told you he (Holmes) was a good geologist (pause), one of the experts on the soils of London.
(Pause) Not to mention their relationship to all kinds of brands of tobacco.
(Pause)
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Jack, maybe we better get off onto another vein.
Surgeon's giving me a puzzled look over here.
We may be getting in trouble.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: You want to talk about veins.
Now that's something an old ore geologist could talk about all night.
(Pause)
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Ore geologists and cardiologists alike.
(Pause)
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Thou strikest for the jugular.
(Pause)
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Jack, we running a contest down here to come up with a reply to that.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: We're getting a request, many requests, for a weather report.
We've been missing your weather reports and wonder what the weather is on the Moon right now.
(Pause)
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Well, the Moon's weather is clear and sunny.
It's only scattered clouds, and all of those seem to be attached to the Earth.
(Pause)
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Except for a cloud of dust around the right rear wheel of the Rover, we've noticed.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Yeah, but that dissipates in the morning warmth.
(Pause) Believe it or not, Joe, I'm going to be off the air briefly.
(Pause)
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: So far, I don't believe that.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Well, if you don't get any heartbeat for a little while, don't worry.
(Pause)
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Joe, we're asleep.
There's no need to answer.
See you in the morning.
transcript.speaker: Cernan
